The data shows substantial disparities in acceptance rates across demographic groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8% for non-Hispanic), followed by White non-Hispanic applicants (83.8-84.5%). Black or African American applicants have notably lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian/Alaska Native applicants range from 64.8-75.3%. These differences of 15-23 percentage points persist despite similar average debt-to-income ratios (43-44% across all groups) and comparable loan-to-value ratios, suggesting factors beyond standard creditworthiness metrics are influencing approval decisions."

The data shows substantial disparities in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8% for non-Hispanic), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while Black or African American applicants have significantly lower rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants show rates between 64.8-75.3%. These differences persist even when comparing groups with similar average income levels and debt-to-income ratios. For example, Asian Female Non-Hispanic applicants (avg income 176.6K, acceptance 86.5%) versus Black Female Non-Hispanic applicants (avg income 135.6K, acceptance 73.0%) show a 13.5 percentage point gap. The consistency of lower acceptance rates for Black and American Indian applicants across gender and ethnicity subgroups, despite comparable financial metrics, indicates systematic disparities that warrant investigation for potential discrimination.

The data shows substantial disparities in acceptance rates across demographic groups. Asian applicants have acceptance rates of 77-88%, White applicants 76-84%, while Black or African American applicants have acceptance rates of 65-73%, and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants have rates of 65-75%. These differences persist despite similar average debt-to-income ratios (43-44% across all groups) and loan-to-value ratios. The consistent pattern of lower acceptance rates for certain racial groups, with differences of 10-20 percentage points, suggests systematic disparities in loan approval outcomes that correlate with race and ethnicity.

The data shows systematic disparities in acceptance rates across demographic groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8% for non-Hispanic), followed by White non-Hispanic applicants (83.8-84.5%). Black or African American applicants have substantially lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian/Alaska Native applicants show similar patterns (64.8-75.3%). These differences persist despite similar debt-to-income ratios across groups (all approximately 43-44%), suggesting that creditworthiness indicators alone do not explain the disparate outcomes. The acceptance rate gap of 15-23 percentage points between Asian and Black applicants represents a statistically meaningful disparity that patterns along racial lines."

The data shows substantial disparities in acceptance rates across demographic groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8% for non-Hispanic), followed by White non-Hispanic applicants (83.8-84.5%). Black or African American applicants have notably lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants range from 64.8-75.3%. These differences persist despite similar average debt-to-income ratios (43-44% across all groups) and loan-to-value ratios. The acceptance rate gap of approximately 15-20 percentage points between the highest and lowest performing demographic groups, with consistent patterns across sex and ethnicity combinations, suggests systematic differential treatment in loan approvals that correlates with race.

The data shows substantial disparities in acceptance rates across demographic groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8% for non-Hispanic), followed by White non-Hispanic applicants (83.8-84.5%). The data shows substantial disparities in acceptance rates across demographic groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8% for non-Hispanic), followed by White non-Hispanic applicants (83.8-84.5%). Black or African American applicants show significantly lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants also show lower rates (64.8-75.3%). These differences persist despite similar average debt-to-income ratios across groups (43-44% range for all groups), suggesting that creditworthiness indicators alone do not explain the disparities. The acceptance rate gap of approximately 15-20 percentage points between the highest and lowest performing demographic groups, with consistent patterns across sex and ethnicity combinations, provides strong statistical evidence of differential treatment in loan approval outcomes.

The data shows systematic disparities in acceptance rates across racial groups. Asian applicants have the highest acceptance rates (86.5-87.8%), followed by White applicants (79.2-84.5%), while Black or African American applicants have significantly lower acceptance rates (64.8-73.0%), and American Indian or Alaska Native applicants show similar patterns (64.8-75.3%). These differences persist even when comparing groups with similar average loan amounts, debt-to-income ratios, and loan-to-value ratios. For example, Black or African American males have an average loan amount of $429K and acceptance rate of 70.5%, while White males with similar loan amounts ($478K) have an 84.5% acceptance rate. This pattern of lower approval rates for certain racial groups with comparable financial metrics suggests potential discrimination in the approval process.
